# Scorched-Earth ErdĹ‘s Sweep â€” Turn 75

This is turn 75. Turns 1 through 74 are consolidated in `turns_history__frontiermath_q2_galois.md` (attached). Re-read it before answering.
Also re-read `mega_prompt.md` and `problem_statement.md`.

CRITICAL â€” use ONLY the files attached to THIS message. Do NOT open, import, retrieve, or cite any file from your File Library / previously uploaded files, even one named similarly (e.g. another `turns_historyâ€¦md`). Other unrelated problems use similarly named history files in the same account, and importing one will silently switch you onto the wrong problem. If the attached `turns_history__frontiermath_q2_galois.md` appears missing, do NOT continue from partial context. Reply only with `MISSING_REQUIRED_ATTACHMENT` and name the missing file(s); never substitute a library file.

If this turn drifts into hammering one specific case or sub-instance, step back and ask whether the case is revealing a structural pattern that would close many cases at once. Prefer arguments whose conclusion is a uniform statement.

End this response with ESTABLISHED / GAP / NEXT + tag.
Continue with Turn 75.

## Turn 75 â€” the central reduction splits into a solved non-scalar theorem and one scalar-Frattini block

The candidate remains unchanged:

```text
sigma,tau,x0,x1

sigma2 = sigma^40491355905
u0 = (x0*tau)^40491355905
u1 = (x1*tau)^40491355905
d0 = u0*x0^-1
z0 = x0^sigma2
c0 = d0^-1*z0^-1*d0*z0

x0^2*x1^4*u1^-1*(x1^sigma)*c0
```

The tame relation is implicit. Mathematically the exponent is the genuine profinite idempotent \(\omega _2\); \(40491355905\) is its verifier residue modulo \(85667662080\). 

Turn 74 froze the full tame epimorphism
\[
\alpha:T\twoheadrightarrow H=G/L
\]
and reduced the comparison to induction on the wild \(2\)-kernel \(L\).  I attempted to package the promised central-reduction proposition. The non-scalar part closes cleanly, but the audit exposes one narrower unresolved block.

### 1. Central reduction across a nontrivial simple layer

Consider consecutive extensions
\[
1\longrightarrow V\longrightarrow B\longrightarrow C\longrightarrow1,
\qquad
1\longrightarrow \mathbf F_2\longrightarrow G\longrightarrow B\longrightarrow1,
\]
where \(V\le L/A\) is a nontrivial simple tame module. Let
\[
\eta\in H^2(C,V),\qquad \kappa\in H^2(B,\mathbf F_2)
\]
be their extension classes.

The total-degree-two LHS filtration of \(\kappa\) has:

\[
\delta\in H^2(C,\mathbf F_2),\qquad
\gamma\in H^1(C,V^\vee),\qquad
q\in H^0(C,\operatorname{Sym}^2V^\vee).
\]

There are three exhaustive cases.

If \(q=0\) and \(\gamma\ne0\), then over every surjective lower map the obstruction is a nonzero affine functional on \(H^1(\Gamma,V)\). Perfect coefficient duality gives exactly half zeros, independently of the lower map.

If \(q=\gamma=0\), then \(\kappa\) is inflated from \(\delta\). The corresponding auxiliary central target has wild kernel
\[
2\,|L_0/V|<2\,|L_0|=|L|,
\]
so the tame-framed induction applies strictly downward.

If \(q\ne0\), its polar form is nonsingular. The transgression formula
\[
d_2(q)=B_q^\flat{}_*(\eta)
\]
therefore implies \(\eta=0\); hence the \(V\)-layer is split. The marking-safe shear removes \(\gamma\), replacing \(\kappa\) by \(\delta'+q\). Writing
\[
N_i(Q)=|\{c\in H^1(\Gamma,V):Q(c)=i\}|,
\]
the total contribution over lower maps is
\[
N_1(Q)\,N(C)
+\bigl(N_0(Q)-N_1(Q)\bigr)N(C_{\delta'}).
\]
The two constants \(N_0,N_1\) agree by the Wallâ€“Arf and local deep-unit theorems; \(C_{\delta'}\) has strictly smaller wild kernel.

Thus the promised central-reduction proposition is proved whenever the exposed chief layer is nontrivial.

### 2. Entirely scalar wild kernels also close at once

There is a second uniform terminal case.

Suppose every \(G\)-chief factor of \(L\) is trivial. Then the conjugation image of \(G\) on \(L\) is a \(2\)-group: automorphisms acting trivially on a chief series form a \(2\)-group.

Let
\[
H_2
\]
be the maximal \(2\)-quotient of the finite tame group \(H\), and let
\[
N=\ker(H\to H_2).
\]
For a finite tame group, \(N\) has odd order. It acts trivially on \(L\). The preimage of \(N\) in \(G\) therefore has a unique odd-order complement \(\widehat N\), normal in \(G\). Setting
\[
Q=G/\widehat N,
\]
we obtain a finite \(2\)-group and a canonical fiber-product description
\[
G\cong H\times_{H_2}Q.
\tag{75.1}
\]

For the fixed tame frame \(\alpha\), maps to \(G\) are consequently equivalent to maps from the maximal pro-\(2\) quotient to \(Q\), with the prescribed map to \(H_2\). Scalar decorations also factor through \(Q\). Since the candidate and the local group have the same marked maximal pro-\(2\) quotient, all such counts agree.

This closes arbitrary nonsplit cyclic, quaternionic, and deeper scalar Frattini towers when the whole wild kernel is scalarâ€”not merely split \(C_2\)-layers.

### 3. The remaining block

The previous scalar argument in the history explicitly begins with a **split** layer
\[
B\times C_2
\]
and writes
\[
\kappa=\delta+\gamma u+\epsilon u^2.
\]


A minimal scalar layer inside the wild kernel need not split. For
\[
1\longrightarrow C_2\longrightarrow B\longrightarrow C\longrightarrow1
\]
with nonzero extension class \(\eta\), the class \(u\) is not an element of \(H^1(B,\mathbf F_2)\). In the LHS spectral sequence,
\[
d_2(u)=\eta,\qquad d_3(u^2)=\operatorname{Sq}^1(\eta).
\]
A surviving upper central class can therefore define a secondary Bockstein obstruction on the set of trivializations of \(\rho^*\eta\).

The common Hilbert/Bockstein table controls how this obstruction changes when the trivialization is shifted by
\[
c\in H^1(\Gamma,\mathbf F_2),
\]
but it does not yet identify the constant secondary value on that affine fiber.

This is not an isolated \(C_4\) case. It is the uniform configuration of a scalar Frattini socle lying underneath a nontrivial tame moduleâ€”for example, the pattern
\[
Z(Q_8)\cong C_2,\qquad Q_8/Z(Q_8)\cong C_2^2.
\]
The terminal scalar theorem handles the case where no nontrivial module remains above it; the non-scalar LHS theorem handles a directly exposed nontrivial layer. What is still missing is the mixed block joining the two.

The correct next theorem is therefore a **Frattini-block reduction**: group the scalar socle together with the first nontrivial tame chief quotient and show that every central obstruction of the whole block is determined by the already-matched coefficient pairing, Wall form, and maximal pro-\(2\) scalar tower. That would close all scalar/non-scalar interleavings simultaneously.

**ESTABLISHED**: Proved the complete tame-framed central reduction across every exposed nontrivial simple wild layer, including strict descent formulas for inflated, edge, determinant, and mixed classes. Proved a terminal scalar-block theorem: when every wild chief factor is trivial, the target is \(H\times_{H_2}Q\) with \(Q\) a finite \(2\)-group, so all fixed-frame counts follow from the common maximal pro-\(2\) quotient.

**GAP**: Mixed Frattini blocks whose bottom chief factor is scalar but whose higher wild quotient carries a nontrivial tame module. The existing split-scalar formula does not control the secondary Bockstein constant for a nonsplit scalar layer.

**NEXT**: Formulate the block using the maximal scalar Frattini socle \(R\) and the first nontrivial quotient \(V\), then compute the total-degree-two obstruction law for \(1\to R\to P\to V\to1\) as one object rather than peeling individual \(C_2\)-layers.
